title: Ax-Schanuel and the Rank One Riemann-Hilbert Correspondence
speaker: Jonathan Pila
abstract: A result of Simpson characterizes subspaces that are (irreducible) algebraic varieties on both sides of the rank one Riemann-Hilbert correspondence. This result suggests an underlying Ax-Schanuel statement. In joint work with Jacob Tsimerman we affirm this and recover the bi-algebraicity result.
tags: Workshop on Recent Developments in Hodge Theory and O-minimality

proof_stages.label: Rank-one Riemann-Hilbert target
proof_stages.summary: The talk identifies the rank-one Riemann-Hilbert correspondence with a biholomorphic group isomorphism from the universal vectorial extension of Pic^0 to a complex torus, and recalls Simpson's theorem that its bi-algebraic subvarieties are subtori/cosets.

proof_stages.label: General null-pair Ax-Lindemann formulation
proof_stages.summary: The rank-one problem is reframed for a biholomorphic group isomorphism theta:R→S between connected commutative complex algebraic groups satisfying a no-oblique-subgroups/null-pair condition, with the desired conclusion that the Zariski closure of theta(V) is a coset.

proof_stages.label: Differential-field Ax-Schanuel paradigm
proof_stages.summary: The speaker sets up differential fields, differential equations characterizing lifts to universal covers, the rank of a tuple, the classical Ax-Schanuel theorem for (C*)^n, and the resulting Ax-Lindemann principle that Zariski closures of exponential images are weakly special.

proof_stages.label: Group Ax-Schanuel with NVQ correction
proof_stages.summary: The functional-transcendence input is extended to connected commutative algebraic groups by using the no-vectorial-quotient part and NVQ dimension, since vector group quotients contribute no covering transcendence.

proof_stages.label: Full-rank graph point and Ax-Schanuel lower bound
proof_stages.summary: For an algebraic subvariety V⊂R, a full-rank differential-field point x∈V and y=theta(x) are placed on the graph of theta, whose lifted map is linear, and Ax-Schanuel for R×S gives a lower bound involving rank(x) and the dimensions of the relevant product coset.

proof_stages.label: Eliminating lifts and forcing the image coset
proof_stages.summary: The possible transcendence loss from forgetting the lifts is bounded by the NVQ dimension of the R-projection, and comparison with the elementary upper bound from V×W forces the Zariski closure W of theta(V) to equal the minimal S-coset, proving the Ax-Lindemann statement and hence Simpson's bi-algebraicity conclusion.

examples.name: Classical algebraic torus and logarithmic lifts
examples.note: The case X=(C*)^n, with lifts given by logarithms satisfying d log x = dx/x, illustrates the rank term, weakly special cosets, and the Ax-Lindemann consequence before the general group theorem is invoked.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker works through the classical torus case by identifying points and lifts, writing the logarithmic differential equation, applying Ax-Schanuel/Ax-Lindemann, and interpreting cosets as linear dependence

examples.name: Additive vector group obstruction
examples.note: Taking G to be a vector/additive group, whose universal covering map is the identity, shows why a naive Ax-Schanuel statement for all commutative algebraic groups must be modified by excluding or quotienting vectorial parts.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker tests the Ax-Schanuel statement on the additive vector group, noting that the covering map is the identity and hence the expected transcendence cannot occur

passes.reason: The additive vector group has identity universal cover and no exponential transcendence, so it is a collapsed vectorial case used to show the naive general theorem fails.
The additive vector group has trivial identity covering, giving too little transcendental structure and showing the theorem cannot hold for arbitrary groups.
The additive vector group has a collapsed identity covering giving no transcendence, and is used as an obstruction to the naive theorem for arbitrary groups.
The pure additive vector group has a collapsed identity covering and is used to show the naive Ax-Schanuel statement fails for arbitrary groups.
the pure additive vector group has identity universal cover and zero non-vectorial part, giving the no-transcendence obstruction to the naive theorem
Taking $G=\mathbb G_a^n$ makes the universal covering map the identity and yields no transcendence, so it is a collapsed vectorial case used to show the naive theorem cannot hold for all groups.
The additive vector group is used to show the Ax-Schanuel statement cannot hold for arbitrary groups, and its identity universal cover gives a collapsed vectorial case with no covering transcendence.
the pure additive vector group has identity universal cover, so it obstructs the unrestricted theorem.
